content:
# Deuteronomy 15 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA Arbiter** | Sources: EMET (E), LOGOS (L), PNEUMA (P)

---

## v.1 — shemittah gloss

- **E:** "you shall make a release [shemittah]"
- **L:** "you shall grant a release" — no Hebrew term
- **P:** "you must cancel debts" — paraphrase, no Hebrew term
- **SOPHIA:** Retains *shemittah* with bracketed gloss per project convention. P's "cancel debts" is interpretively strong but domesticates the breadth of the Hebrew concept.

---

## v.2 — "creditor" rendering

- **E:** "every creditor shall release what he has lent" (renders *ba'al masheh* as "creditor")
- **L:** "every creditor shall release what he has lent" — same
- **P:** "every creditor who has made a loan to a neighbor shall release the claim"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts E/L's conciser rendering.

---

## v.3 — "foreigner" and force of the command

- **E:** "The foreigner you may press"
- **L:** "Of a foreigner you may exact it"
- **P:** "You may press a foreigner to pay"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "Of a foreigner you may press payment" (blend E/L/P) — adds "payment" for clarity, adopts "press" for the Hebrew *nagas*.

---

## v.5 — "listen carefully / fully obey" vs. "hearken diligently"

- **E:** "only if you listen carefully to the voice of the LORD"
- **L:** "if only you fully obey the voice of the LORD your God"
- **P:** "if only you fully obey the LORD your God"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "if only you fully obey the voice of the LORD your God" (L).

---

## v.7 — "poor man" vs. "anyone who is poor"

- **E:** "a poor man, one of your brothers"
- **L:** "a poor man — one of your brothers"
- **P:** "anyone among you is poor — one of your fellow Israelites"
- **SOPHIA:** Retains "a poor man, one of your brothers" (E/L). P's "fellow Israelites" departs from the brotherly *ach* language throughout the passage.

---

## v.9 — "belial / worthless thing" as noun vs. "worthless thought" vs. "wicked thought"

- **E:** "a word in your heart, a worthless [belial] thing"
- **L:** "a worthless thought in your heart"
- **P:** "this wicked thought take hold of you"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "a worthless thought in your heart" (L). E's bracket on *belial* is noted; P's "wicked thought" implies a moral category heavier than the Hebrew warrants here.

---

## v.9 — "your eye is evil" vs. "your eye is hostile" vs. "cold eye"

- **E:** "your eye is evil against your poor brother"
- **L:** "your eye is hostile toward your poor brother"
- **P:** "you look at your poor neighbor with a cold eye" (in notes only)
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "your eye is hostile toward your poor brother" (L) as preserving the idiom without being opaque ("evil eye") or overly dynamic ("cold eye").

---

## v.11 — "afflicted and poor" vs. "needy and poor"

- **E:** "to your afflicted and to your poor in your land" (*anaw* and *evyon* distinguished)
- **L:** "to the needy and to the poor in your land"
- **P:** "who are poor and needy in your land" (order reversed)
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "to the needy and to the poor in your land" (L). E's preservation of both distinct terms is noted as a textual observation.

---

## v.12 — "serves you six years" vs. "is sold to you and serves you six years"

- **E:** "is sold to you and serves you six years"
- **L:** "is sold to you, he shall serve you for six years"
- **P:** "sells themselves to you and serves you six years"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "is sold to you and serves you six years" (E). P's "sells themselves" is a reflexive rendering that, while a possible meaning, introduces a different legal nuance.

---

## v.14 — "adorn him" vs. "furnish him liberally" vs. "supply them generously"

- **E:** "you shall surely adorn him" (literal *ha'aneq ta'aniq*)
- **L:** "you shall furnish him liberally"
- **P:** "Supply them generously"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "You shall furnish him liberally" (L). E's "adorn" is technically accurate for the cognate construction but misleading in English. P's plural is inconsistent with the MT singular.

---

## v.17 — "servant" vs. "slave"

- **E:** "a servant forever" (*eved*)
- **L:** "your slave forever"
- **P:** "your servant for life"
- **SOPHIA:** "your servant forever" — follows E and the project's general approach to *eved* in indentured-labor contexts. L's "slave" is the stronger word but may overstate the nature of this voluntary, permanent arrangement. P's "for life" correctly interprets *'olam* contextually.

---

## v.18 — "double the wage" vs. "half the cost" — significant divergence

- **E:** "at double the wage of a hired worker he has served you six years"
- **L:** "at half the cost of a hired worker he has served you six years"
- **P:** "their service to you has been worth twice what you would have paid a hired hand"
- **SOPHIA:** Significant disagreement. E and P both render *mishneh* as "double/twice." L renders as "half the cost." The Hebrew *mishneh sekir sakir* most naturally means "double the hire of a day-laborer" — i.e., the servant has given you twice the value. SOPHIA follows E/P: "at double the wage of a hired worker."

---

## v.19 — "consecrate" vs. "set apart"

- **E:** "you shall consecrate [taqdiish]"
- **L:** "you shall consecrate"
- **P:** "Set apart for the LORD"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "you shall consecrate" (E/L). P's "set apart" is correct but loses the specifically sacrificial-holiness connotation of *qadash*.

---

## v.21 — "severe blemish" vs. "serious blemish" vs. "serious flaw"

- **E:** "any severe blemish"
- **L:** "any serious blemish"
- **P:** "any serious flaw"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "any serious blemish" (L). "Flaw" (P) is fine but "blemish" maintains the sacrificial technical register of *mum*.

---

*Total disagreements logged: 13*
